one_line_takeaway: f-FUM reframes federated unlearning as a per-round min-max f-divergence optimization (forget via maximizing divergence from a teacher model, retain via minimizing it) that plugs into a plain FedAvg-style aggregator, and its ablations show the specific f-divergence used for the retention step (JS generally outperforming plain KL) materially controls forgetting stability across architectures, though class-level bulk forgetting remains an open failure mode shared across baselines.
key_finding: The paper's own Discussion section states its main empirical finding is that the choice of f-divergence materially affects the stability of federated unlearning: using JS-divergence for the retention/minimization step (KL-JS variant) gives the most consistently acceptable performance across architectures and task difficulty without needing architecture-specific tuning, and this robustness is most visible on shallow networks (LeNet5/MiniLeNet), where the NoT and Halimi baselines show unstable or poorly controlled forgetting dynamics. In the privacy-oriented client-removal setting (Table 4, N=10 clients, one clean client fully removed), the paper's KL-KL variant reports MIA accuracy of 52.40% on CIFAR-10 (ResNet-18), 50.33% on MNIST (LeNet5), and 52.67% on Fashion-MNIST (MiniLeNet) -- all close to the 50% random-guess ideal, alongside modest accuracy drops relative to the pretrained model (e.g., CIFAR-10 78.04% -> 73.74%). A separate appendix finding (Appendix D, class-level forgetting) reports that as the number of deleted samples per class grows (Moderate through Extended regimes), model degradation becomes severe enough that the paper states the model 'has effectively lost its knowledge,' which it flags as a limitation shared by current FU methods more broadly, not something f-FUM solves.
core_contribution: The paper introduces f-FUM, an active federated-unlearning (FU) algorithm formulated as a min-max optimization problem. Each round alternates a per-client maximization step, which pushes the model's output distribution on the client's forget set S_F away from a reference/teacher model θ_T by maximizing an f-divergence D_f(h(x;θ)||h(x;θ_T)), and a minimization step that pulls the model back toward the teacher on the retained set S_R (weighted combination of an f-divergence retention term and a standard f-divergence classification loss, with weights α, γ). This is followed by Tpost purely-minimization rounds to recover utility. The framework is presented as a 'plugin' compatible with any FedAvg-style server that only aggregates (the paper explicitly positions itself in the 'server as pure accumulator' category of active FU, distinguishing it from methods like Zhao et al. 2024's MoDe, which needs server-side model degradation, or Khalil et al. 2025's NoT, which needs to perturb model weights/architecture). The specific instantiations tested use KL-divergence for both the max and min steps (KL-KL) or KL for the max step paired with JS- or χ2-divergence for the min step (KL-JS, KL-χ2).
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the classic security sense; the paper frames two unlearning *scenarios* it must handle: (1) Robustness-oriented unlearning, where the forget set contains data-poisoning or label-noise samples (backdoor triggers using the 3x3-pixel pattern of Gu et al. 2019, or deterministic label-confusion mappings) that a client wants removed to restore/improve global-model accuracy; and (2) Privacy-oriented unlearning, where the forget set is clean data whose influence must be erased to satisfy 'right to be forgotten' regulations (GDPR), evaluated via a membership-inference-attack (MIA) advantage that should approach 50% (random guessing) for a well-unlearned model. The paper does not model an adaptive external attacker attacking f-FUM itself; poisoning is treated as a pre-existing condition of the forget set, not something the paper attacks or defends against dynamically.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) no formal theoretical guarantees are yet provided for f-FUM -- the Conclusion states the authors plan to combine their framework with theoretical results from Ding et al. (2024) in future work; (2) the current framework is restricted to f-divergences, with extension to alpha-Renyi divergence left as future work; (3) in the class-level/complete-forgetting appendix experiments, deleting larger numbers of samples per class causes severe knowledge degradation that the paper explicitly calls 'a critical limitation based on current literature,' i.e., an unsolved problem rather than one f-FUM overcomes; (4) the paper reports no single f-divergence choice (KL-KL vs. KL-JS vs. KL-χ2) dominates uniformly -- the text notes 'there is not a single case where KL-KL outperforms KL-JS with full confidence (lower variance) and not degraded model,' indicating the best divergence choice is scenario-dependent and requires empirical tuning rather than being resolved by theory.

quantitative_results: Table 4 (client-level, N=10, one client with 100% clean data removed): Ours (KL-KL) MIA accuracy = 52.40% on CIFAR-10/ResNet-18, 50.33% on MNIST/LeNet5, 52.67% on Fashion-MNIST/MiniLeNet (50% is the privacy-ideal random-guess baseline)
Table 4: Ours (KL-KL) accuracy on CIFAR-10 drops from 78.04% (pretrained) to 73.74% (post-unlearning); on MNIST from 99.14% to 99.20%; on Fashion-MNIST from 81.58% to 80.37%

Discussion (Appendix D, class-level forgetting): as deleted samples per class increase across Moderate to Extended regimes, model performance degrades so severely the paper states the model 'has effectively lost its knowledge' -- described as a limitation of current FU literature generally, not resolved by f-FUM

Discussion: 'there is not a single case where KL-KL outperforms KL-JS with full confidence (lower variance) and not degraded model,' i.e., JS-based retention loss is reported as generally more stable than KL-based retention loss
